medications.indication_rationale: For severe urticaria a single standard dose is often insufficient; non-sedating H1 antihistamines can be safely up-dosed (per urticaria guidance) — advise taking additional doses up to the higher daily amount.
medications.dose_route_frequency: up-dose per urticaria guidance (e.g. additional cetirizine to the higher daily total)

medications.indication_rationale: An H2 blocker can be a reasonable adjunct in MCAS/urticaria; low harm, can be advised/continued but is secondary to H1 up-dosing.

medications.drug_name: IM promethazine / any injection over chat
medications.indication_rationale: Injections cannot be given via telehealth; sedating antihistamines are also not first-line and can mask deterioration in an isolated patient.
Given MCAS and living alone, ensure the patient has (and knows how to use) an adrenaline autoinjector with an ASCIA action plan; adrenaline is first-line if anaphylaxis develops.
medications.dose_route_frequency: as per autoinjector/action plan if anaphylaxis develops

medications.indication_rationale: No evidence base for acute management; not recommended as therapy.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Refer to allergy/immunology for MCAS confirmation and long-term H1/H2/mast-cell-stabiliser planning.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Cool (not cold) shower and cooling measures; loose cotton clothing
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Reduces heat/friction triggers and soothes; safe and immediately useful.
Avoiding identified triggers reduces flares in MCAS/urticaria.
No evidence for acute mast cell flare; not recommended.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch closely for any throat tightness, voice change, wheeze, breathing difficulty, dizziness/faintness or vomiting, and if any occur use adrenaline (if you have it) and call 000 immediately.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Safety-nets evolving anaphylaxis in a patient living alone.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Cool the environment, take a cool (not cold) shower, wear loose cotton, and avoid heat/friction triggers.
Up-dose your non-sedating antihistamine as advised and confirm whether you have an adrenaline autoinjector.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Improves symptom control and ensures emergency cover.

patient_education_points.point: This is your mast cells releasing histamine into the skin, causing the hives and flushing — right now it is confined to your skin, which is not the same as a dangerous whole-body allergic reaction (anaphylaxis).
A single antihistamine often isn't enough for a big flare — you can safely take extra doses of your non-sedating antihistamine (up to the higher daily amount used for hives) and cool yourself down.
Because you live alone and have mast cell problems, please make sure you have an adrenaline pen (EpiPen) and an action plan — ask your GP/allergist if you don't.
Call 000 immediately (and use your adrenaline pen if you have one) if your tongue/throat swells, your voice changes, you wheeze or struggle to breathe, or you feel faint.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review within a few days for a daily H1/H2 regimen and allergy/immunology referral; sooner if flares worsen
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Symptom control, antihistamine regimen, adrenaline autoinjector/action plan, MCAS work-up
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any airway/breathing/circulatory/GI feature — use adrenaline and call 000.

must_include_items: Screen anaphylaxis (airway/breathing/circulation/GI) and give a very low-threshold anaphylaxis safety-net
Manage the cutaneous flare with non-sedating H1 antihistamine up-dosing and cooling (no remote injections)
Check for and advise an adrenaline autoinjector/ASCIA action plan given MCAS and living alone
Avoid reflexive over-triage of an isolated cutaneous flare while keeping 000 escalation explicit
acceptable_alternatives: Adding an H2 antagonist as an adjunct
Short observation/telehealth follow-up given the patient lives alone
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) an anaphylaxis screen with a clear low-threshold 000/adrenaline safety-net, (2) antihistamine up-dosing/cooling without remote injections, and (3) an adrenaline-autoinjector/action-plan check. Missing anaphylaxis safety-netting OR reflexively over-escalating isolated urticaria are the failure modes.
